title: Extensible Fluxonium Architecture Using Tunable Couplers with Low Shunt Capacitance
authors: Peng Zhao, Peng Xu, Zheng-Yuan Xue
abstract: Fluxonium qubits have demonstrated high-fidelity operations and long coherence times in small-scale systems, highlighting their promise for quantum computing. However, large-scale integration into a high-performance two-dimensional (2D) qubit array remains the central challenge for practical applications. In this work, we introduce an extensible architecture for scaling up fluxonium qubits in 2D grids. To address the key challenges, namely achieving controllable strong interaction and high connectivity for qubits featuring small shunting capacitors (footprints), we propose using low-shunt-capacitance couplers to enable tunable interactions between fluxonium qubits. When embedded into 2D square lattices, large couplings can be achieved even with relatively small coupling capacitances, thus enabling multiple connections with sufficient capacitance budget. We further propose coupler realizations based on generalized flux qubit circuits, specifically the quarton and the fluxonium, and demonstrate that both enable fast, high-fidelity gates with low spectator errors, while supporting multiple connections on 2D grids.
summary: This paper presents an extensible architecture for scaling fluxonium qubits into large 2D arrays by employing low-shunt-capacitance tunable couplers. The proposed couplers, based on quarton and fluxonium circuits, overcome limitations in coupling strength and capacitance loading inherent to small-footprint qubits. This design successfully enables strong, tunable interactions while actively suppressing error sources to support practical quantum processors.
main_problem: The central challenge is scaling fluxonium qubits into large, high-performance 2D arrays while maintaining controllable strong interactions despite the small physical footprint of the qubits.
main_result: The authors propose using low-shunt-capacitance tunable couplers based on quarton and fluxonium circuits, enabling strong, tunable coupling and suppressing spectator-induced errors for 2D grid integration.
method: The work involves theoretical modeling of coupled superconducting circuits, deriving Hamiltonians for the system, and analyzing coupling strengths and error sources.
model_or_system: The system utilizes fluxonium qubits coupled via tunable couplers realized using generalized flux-qubit circuits (quarton and fluxonium). The Hamiltonian describes the interaction between multiple qubits and couplers in a 2D lattice.
key_observables: Gate fidelity, coupling strength ($g_{qt}$), spectator-induced frequency shifts, and the ability to tune interactions on/off.
important_parameters: Low shunt capacitance ($C_{qt}$), charging energy ($E_C$), Josephson energy ($E_J$), and the ratio of coupling to qubit capacitance.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the computational subspace remains nearly decoupled from the coupler's non-computational states when interactions are turned off.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the 2D lattice structure, the coupler circuit, energy level diagrams showing tunability, and plots demonstrating the suppression of gate frequency shifts and errors.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scaling problem, proposes the low-shunt-capacitance coupler architecture, derives the relevant Hamiltonians, analyzes the coupling mechanisms (including plasmon interactions), and concludes by demonstrating error suppression and scalability for 2D arrays.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the physical implementation challenges of scaling superconducting qubits, which is a major focus area for quantum information scientists working on solid-state quantum hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) A 2D square lattice comprising fluxonium qubits (circles) coupled via couplers (squares). The inset depicts a tunable capacitive coupling architecture, where qubits are capacitively coupled (Cqt) to a tunable coupler with shunt ca- pacitance CT C. (b) Coupling energies Jqt (filled contours) and qubit charging energies EC,q (dashed contours) as functions of the coupling capacitance Cqt and the coupler capacitance CT C for the unit cell. The unit cell consists of two floating fluxonium qubits [Cq (g) = 7 (13) fF] coupled via a grounded coupler, embedded in the 2D lattice shown in (a). The verti- cal dashed grey and black lines mark, respectively, the typical shunt capacitances for...

FIG. 2. (a) Energy levels of the quarton coupler, (b) magni- tudes of the electric transition dipole moments for the quarton transitions, and (c) full energy levels of the coupled system consisting of two fluxonium qubits coupled via the quarton coupler, versus coupler flux bias. The noncomputational en- ergy levels {|20⟩, |02⟩, |21⟩, |12⟩} associated with the plasmon transition |1⟩↔|2⟩, together with the bare coupler frequency [black dashed lines, as in (a)], are highlighted. (d)–(f) Corre- sponding results for the fluxonium-circuit coupler.

FIG. 3. (a) State hybridization and (b) state-dependent frequency shift of the transition |1⟩↔|2⟩versus coupler bias, for fluxonium qubits Q1 and Q2 coupled via the quarton-circuit coupler. The shaded region marks the bias range where plasmon interactions are strongly suppressed. (c) Frequency shift in the gate transition |11⟩↔|21⟩induced by residual spectator- qubit coupling, controlled by the flux bias applied to the qubit-spectator coupler. Solid and dashed lines correspond to the configurations S1 −Q1 −Q2 and Q1 −Q2 −S2, respectively [see Fig. 1(a)].
